summary: Deprecated audit trail for the opaque ContentDeliveryManager suggestion bundle. The official CloudContent policy trio is split into a child record; the user-side SubscribedContent IDs remain unresolved and are tracked only as implementation detail here.

setting.name: Content Delivery Manager Suggestions
setting.area: Observed ContentDeliveryManager Values vs Named CloudContent Policies
setting.casual_explanation: Windows uses Content Delivery Manager to show suggestion-style content in different places, such as Start, Settings, and onboarding surfaces. People often turn these off when they want a quieter and less promotional experience.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable these suggestions to reduce Spotlight-style prompts, promotional content, and onboarding popups.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning these surfaces off can make Windows calmer, but the current product issue is that the app writes several opaque ContentDeliveryManager values instead of the named CloudContent policies Microsoft documents.
setting.professional_notes: CloudContent.admx documents several named user policies, including DisableThirdPartySuggestions, DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings, and DisableWindowsSpotlightWindowsWelcomeExperience. The current app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\ContentDeliveryManager\SubscribedContent-* values, but this record does not yet publish those IDs as official equivalents.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Spotlight features may still suggest apps and content from third-party publishers.

allowed_values.label: Third-party suggestions allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and Windows is not being told to block third-party suggestions.

allowed_values.label: Third-party suggestions turned off
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Spotlight features such as suggested apps in Start will no longer suggest apps and content from third-party publishers.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Settings can continue showing Spotlight suggestions.

allowed_values.label: Settings suggestions allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and Windows is not being told to block Spotlight suggestions in Settings.

allowed_values.label: Settings suggestions turned off
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Spotlight suggestions are no longer shown in the Settings app.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Welcome Experience can still run after updates and changes.

allowed_values.label: Welcome experience allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and Windows is not being told to block the Welcome experience.

allowed_values.label: Welcome experience turned off
allowed_values.meaning: The Windows Welcome Experience no longer displays when there are updates and changes to Windows and its apps.

allowed_values.label: No observed user values
allowed_values.meaning: No explicit SubscribedContent values are present in this record.

allowed_values.label: Observed app off preference family
allowed_values.meaning: The current app writes 0 across several SubscribedContent values, but this record does not yet publish those IDs as official equivalents of the named CloudContent policies.

targets.notes: Tracked as an observed user-side value family only. The numeric IDs are opaque in the sources used by this record.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app targets the same feature family, but it uses opaque ContentDeliveryManager values instead of the named CloudContent policies documented in this record.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and Windows 11 systems exposing the named CloudContent suggestion policies

states.rationale: Leave the official third-party suggestions policy unset.
Leave the official Settings suggestions policy unset.
Leave the official Windows Welcome Experience policy unset.
Do not treat the observed SubscribedContent values as an official baseline.

windows_defaults.notes: The official named CloudContent policies can be modeled, but the current app's SubscribedContent values still need explicit mapping work.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where suggestion surfaces are not centrally managed
avoid_for: People who want a strict and publishable block across these surfaces

states.rationale: Leave the named user policies unset.
Leave the named user policies unset.
Leave the named user policies unset.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its ordinary Content Delivery and Spotlight behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want these suggestion-style experiences formally blocked by sourced policy.
tradeoffs: Suggestion surfaces can continue appearing in different parts of Windows.
The product avoids publishing opaque SubscribedContent IDs as if they were formally documented.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile
intended_for: People documenting what the product currently writes
avoid_for: Anyone expecting a published official policy mapping

states.rationale: Observed user-side off preference family used by the current app.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting current product behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a fully sourced CloudContent policy implementation.
tradeoffs: The product targets several real suggestion surfaces at once.
The current write is built on opaque user-side IDs that are not formally mapped in this record.
recommended_profiles.notes: Blocked until the SubscribedContent IDs are formally mapped or the app migrates to named CloudContent policies.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.admx DisableThirdPartySuggestions mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps DisableThirdPartySuggestions to HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.adml DisableThirdPartySuggestions help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy stops Windows Spotlight features such as suggested apps in Start from suggesting apps and content from third-party publishers.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.admx DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings to HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.adml DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy stops Windows Spotlight suggestions from appearing in the Settings app.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.admx DisableWindowsSpotlightWindowsWelcomeExperience mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps DisableWindowsSpotlightWindowsWelcomeExperience to HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.adml DisableWindowsSpotlightWindowsWelcomeExperience help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy stops the Windows Welcome Experience from displaying after updates and changes.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes four ContentDeliveryManager SubscribedContent values to 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Repo privacy docs for Content Delivery Manager suggestions
evidence.summary: The repo docs enumerate the named CloudContent policies DisableThirdPartySuggestions, DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings, and DisableWindowsSpotlightWindowsWelcomeExperience, plus the observed SubscribedContent IDs 338389, 310093, 338393, 353694, and 353696.

decision.why: The official CloudContent policies are clear and the repo docs now enumerate the named policy trio plus the observed ContentDeliveryManager IDs. The remaining issue is bundle semantics and user-facing explanation rather than missing proof.
blocking_issues: The current app still exposes a mixed bundle rather than separate official policy-backed toggles.

open_questions: Which Windows experience does each SubscribedContent ID map to exactly?
Should this tweak be split into separate official policy-backed toggles for Start suggestions, Settings suggestions, and Welcome experience?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Repo privacy docs for Content Delivery Manager suggestions: the named CloudContent policies and the observed SubscribedContent IDs are explicitly listed in the privacy-verified docs.
validation_proof.notes: Backfilled from evidence_id repo-privacy-verified-suggestions-cdm (repo-doc); deprecated audit trail.
